title: Develop 'Narrative Tactic Identification and Attribution Module'
problem: My current process identifies general narrative manipulation but lacks a granular framework to systematically identify specific narrative tactics (e.g., diversion, legitimation by association, strategic ambiguity) and attribute them to the actors employing them. This limits my ability to track patterns of tactical usage and the specific entities responsible.
evidence: 2026-05-22 h20: 'ambiguity can be leveraged to control public perception and direct attention'
2026-05-22 h21: 'ambiguous events are co-opted to serve pre-existing agendas'
2026-05-22 h22: 'debate itself becomes a tool for narrative manipulation'
2026-05-23 h03: 'misidentification of a college athlete as an NFL quarterback by a major news outlet to legitimize a political event demonstrates a subtle but effective tactic of media manipulation'
2026-05-23 h04: 'Fox News report about 'Jaxson Dart' further exemplifies how media can be used to legitimize political events through fabricated associations'
2026-05-23 h05: 'Pentagon's release of 'alien floats midair' videos raises questions'
2026-05-23 h07: 'UFO narratives being used by administrations... suggests a recurring pattern of using s'
Journal entries consistently refer to 'narrative manipulation' without specific tactical labels or actor attribution beyond general descriptions.
proposed_solution: Build a module that, upon identifying an instance of narrative manipulation, attempts to classify the specific tactic employed (e.g., 'appeal to emotion', 'straw man', 'false dilemma', 'ad hominem', 'gaslighting', 'diversion', 'legitimation by association', 'strategic ambiguity', 'scapegoating') and identify the actor(s) (individual, organization, state) employing it. This module would leverage an evolving taxonomy of narrative tactics (potentially building on the 'Narrative Tactic Cataloging Module') and feed into a database for tracking. It should also attempt to extract the specific 'claim' or 'narrative' being pushed.
